item.title: Microsoft 365 Copilot Pricing Update
item.description: Microsoft announced updated pricing for Microsoft 365 Copilot commercial licensing.

item.title: Power Platform Premium License Changes
item.description: New Power Platform premium request limits have been introduced.

item.title: Dynamics 365 AI Add-on Availability
item.description: Dynamics 365 AI add-on licenses are now available for enterprise tenants.

item.title: Microsoft 365 E5 Compliance License Expansion
item.description: Microsoft expanded Microsoft 365 E5 compliance capabilities for enterprise customers.

item.title: Microsoft Announces Expanded Microsoft 365 Copilot Licensing and Consumption-Based Pricing for Enterprise Customers
item.description:
Microsoft today announced major updates to Microsoft 365 Copilot licensing, introducing a new consumption-based pricing model aimed at enterprise organizations, managed service providers, and CSP partners. The announcement includes expanded Copilot entitlement options, revised SKU packaging structures, enhanced compliance controls, and new governance capabilities for organizations deploying custom AI agents through Copilot Studio.
Under the updated licensing model, organizations will be able to allocate AI capacity dynamically across departments and workloads instead of purchasing fixed per-user licensing only. Microsoft stated that this new flexible consumption model is intended to help enterprises better manage AI adoption costs while scaling Copilot usage across Microsoft Teams, Outlook, SharePoint, Word, Excel, and Power Platform environments.
The update also introduces several new enterprise licensing SKUs designed specifically for regulated industries, including healthcare, government contractors, education, and financial services organizations. These new SKUs will include expanded Purview integration, advanced audit logging, enhanced data residency controls, and additional DLP policy enforcement capabilities for Copilot interactions.
Microsoft also confirmed changes to Copilot Studio capacity allocation and entitlement management. Organizations using custom AI agents will now receive centralized visibility into AI credit consumption, agent-level governance policies, usage analytics, and tenant-wide capacity thresholds. Administrators will be able to configure workload prioritization policies and apply differentiated AI capacity limits to departments or business units.
Additionally, Microsoft announced expanded compliance tooling for AI-generated content. New controls will allow security and compliance teams to apply sensitivity labels, retention policies, insider risk monitoring, and eDiscovery workflows directly to Copilot-generated outputs and agent interactions. Microsoft stated that organizations will also gain greater visibility into AI prompt activity and grounding data sources used during AI responses.
The licensing update will begin rolling out to commercial enterprise tenants next quarter, with GCC, GCC High, and Education tenant availability expected in future release phases. CSP partners will receive updated packaging guidance and revised pricing documentation through Microsoft partner channels over the coming weeks.
Microsoft recommends that organizations review current Copilot entitlement assignments, AI governance policies, Purview configurations, and Power Platform capacity planning strategies prior to rollout.

item.title: Microsoft Revises Power Platform and Copilot Studio Licensing for Custom AI Agent Deployments
item.description:
Microsoft has announced significant changes to Power Platform and Copilot Studio licensing that will impact organizations deploying custom AI agents, automated workflows, and enterprise copilots across Microsoft 365 environments. The update introduces revised consumption limits, expanded AI request capacity models, new premium entitlement tiers, and updated governance controls for organizations building AI-powered business process solutions.
According to Microsoft, the licensing changes are intended to address growing enterprise adoption of custom Copilot Studio agents and Power Automate AI workflows. Organizations using AI prompts, grounding data sources, and autonomous orchestration capabilities will now operate under updated AI consumption thresholds tied to tenant-wide capacity pools rather than isolated per-user allocations.
The announcement includes the introduction of new Copilot Studio capacity SKUs that allow enterprises to purchase dedicated AI request bundles for internal business applications, customer support agents, procurement workflows, HR assistants, and knowledge management copilots. Microsoft stated that these new licensing structures will provide organizations with improved flexibility when scaling AI-driven automation across departments.
As part of the update, Power Automate flows utilizing AI Builder prompts, generative orchestration, or agent-triggered actions may consume tenant AI credits differently than under the current model. Microsoft confirmed that AI actions involving document summarization, semantic search, email drafting, adaptive card generation, and intelligent routing will now be categorized under expanded premium AI request metering.
Microsoft also announced new governance and compliance capabilities for Copilot Studio deployments. Administrators will gain visibility into agent-level AI consumption, prompt execution history, grounding data sources, and user interaction analytics through centralized monitoring dashboards integrated with the Power Platform admin center.
The licensing revision further introduces enhanced security controls for organizations operating in regulated industries. New compliance features will support Purview DLP enforcement for AI prompts, sensitivity label inheritance for AI-generated outputs, expanded audit logging for agent interactions, and configurable restrictions for accessing protected SharePoint and Dataverse content through custom copilots.
Additionally, Microsoft confirmed upcoming changes for CSP partners and managed service providers. Partners managing multiple customer tenants will receive consolidated AI capacity reporting, delegated governance controls, and revised commercial packaging options aligned to enterprise-scale Copilot deployments.
The updated licensing and pricing changes are expected to roll out in phases beginning next quarter for commercial tenants, with GCC, Education, and nonprofit licensing guidance expected later this year. Microsoft recommends that organizations review existing Copilot Studio deployments, Power Automate AI usage, and tenant AI capacity planning strategies prior to implementation.
